directions.name: Register-first PTX pair export that shrinks hot-band c_shared scratch
directions.hypothesis: Round 3 made the key point for ranking: asymmetric handoff retime can recover from a bad branch by slashing mio, but it still lost to the accepted base because barrier and long-scoreboard both moved the wrong way. That says schedule-only retiming is mostly redistributing stalls, not removing work. The next highest-upside move inside the 64x384 PTX mainline is to attack the remaining export round-trip directly: keep the unchanged 64x96 tail and full-width hot-band compute, but push the paired export toward register-packed BF16 draining so the hot kernel depends less on float c_shared scratch and its warp-sync/export walk. Even a partial collapse of c_shared can cut LSU/shared traffic now and create real shared-memory headroom for deeper overlap later.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Export-side shared traffic and scratch allocation are still taxing the hot-band kernel; they show up indirectly through LSU pressure, scoreboard exposure, and lack of real overlap headroom rather than through mio alone.
directions.risk: Medium-high risk. A more direct export path can raise register pressure, complicate correctness, or fail to shrink shared usage enough to matter. It is still ranked first because it is materially different from the failed retime and helper-compaction families, and it offers both immediate payoff and a path to larger overlap changes later in the 20-round branch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared allocation stays effectively flat and shared/LSU pressure do not fall, or if register growth erases any export-side gain.

directions.name: True third A/B stage on the hot band, funded by export-budget recovery
directions.hypothesis: The accepted base and round 3 both remain stuck at occupancy_limit_registers=1 with long-scoreboard in the 6-plus range, which means the branch still lacks real latency-hiding depth. A materially different follow-up from the failed handoff-retime family is to create actual extra overlap instead of retiming the same two stages: reduce or remove enough hot-band c_shared scratch to fit a third A/B shared stage under the 48 KiB block budget, then run the peeled PTX kernel with a genuine three-stage cp.async steady state. This is not 'another retime'; it is a real pipeline-depth change made possible by reallocating shared memory away from the export path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The hot-band kernel is still limited by insufficient overlap depth under one-block occupancy, not just by the precise ordering of the current two-stage handoff.
directions.risk: High risk and larger scope. The static shared-memory math has to close first, and a third stage can backfire if it adds barrier pressure without enough additional overlap. It is ranked below dir_01 because it likely depends on first reclaiming export scratch budget or otherwise shrinking the current shared footprint materially.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the shared-memory budget cannot support a real third A/B stage, or if the added stage raises barrier pressure without a clear reduction in long-scoreboard.

directions.name: Full-width PTX B-fragment lookahead inside the 12-tile sweep
directions.hypothesis: If the remaining underfeed is more local than the CTA handoff, the next bounded PTX-local lever is inside the 12-tile accumulation walk itself. Keep the full-width 64x384 hot-band branch and unchanged 64x96 tail, but restructure ptx_wmma_accumulate_tile_set_384() so one next B fragment is loaded ahead while the current tile's mma is issued. That would attack the shared-to-fragment load/use chain directly without retrying the rejected explicit half-panel compute rewrite, without revisiting panelized B reorder, and without returning to helper-lifetime cleanup as the main lever.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual long-scoreboard latency may now be dominated by the warp-local B-fragment load/use sequence inside each K-slice rather than by global stage timing.
directions.risk: Medium-high risk. A lookahead B-fragment can push registers up, and it sits closer to the already-low-value issue-order family than the first two directions. It stays ranked because it is still a distinct mechanism from the rejected handoff-retime and helper-compaction paths.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if long-scoreboard does not fall together with tensor activity, or if the extra fragment lookahead pushes register pressure up without a runtime win.

notes: All three directions stay inside the 64x384 hot-band PTX mainline and keep the 64x96 tail unchanged. Ranking is anchored on the restored accepted base 20260419_142213_bf16_gemm_v1_9bdc160 and the latest diagnosed run 20260419_171842_bf16_gemm_v1_74f163a. The explicit lesson from round 3 is that asymmetric two-stage handoff retime is not the right family to keep pushing: it recovered most of the round-2 loss by driving mio down, but it still trailed the accepted base because barrier and long-scoreboard rose. Negative evidence carried forward remains strict: no explicit half-panel mma.sync compute rewrite, no pair-compaction retry, no panelized B-load reorder, no helper/lifetime compaction retry as the main lever, and no near-neighbor handoff-retime retry. The recommended direction therefore shifts to removing real hot-path work and shared-footprint from the export side, which is both materially different and more likely to unlock the next layer of overlap.
